questions.question: Are AI agents the next big thing?
questions.answer: This is the widest Frontier Gap on our entire board: the science panel rates agent feasibility 91/100 while the software proxy sits at its 16th percentile — a Gap of +76. Expert conviction: 93/100, rising. High feasibility, unpriced trade — exactly the profile our system exists to find.

questions.question: Are datacenter stocks overvalued?
questions.answer: By trade extension, no: the power/datacenter basket sits at the 35th percentile of its 10-year history while our science panel rates the underlying demand story 84/100. Compare the chip complex at its 98th percentile — that's where the froth concentrated.

questions.question: Are energy stocks a good buy in 2026?
questions.answer: Expert conviction: 69/100. The thesis is the AI buildout as an energy shock — datacenters colliding with a decade of underinvestment. The falsifier: hyperscalers cutting capex two quarters running.

questions.question: Are GLP-1 stocks still a buy?
questions.answer: The science panel rates the GLP-1 story 76/100 (durable, expanding indications) while the trade sits at the 55th percentile — a Frontier Gap of +21: still half-priced by our measure. Expert conviction: 91/100. Our LLY and NVO deep dives carry the per-name verdicts.

questions.question: Are humanoid robots the next big market?
questions.answer: The science panel rates physical AI feasibility 77/100 and expert conviction runs 97/100, rising — but there's no clean public-market vehicle yet, so we publish no Gap. It sits on our 'frontier with no vehicle' list: when the vehicle appears, the Gap is born wide.

questions.question: Are stablecoins the real crypto story?
questions.answer: Quietly, yes — expert conviction on stablecoins & tokenization runs 89/100 (534 claims and growing fast). The thesis: dollar rails on crypto infrastructure are the adoption vector nobody protests — remittances, settlement, and Treasury demand in one wrapper.

questions.question: Are we heading for a recession in 2026?
questions.answer: Not the base case among the voices we track: equities conviction holds at 66/100 and the regime read is reflation tipping toward inflation, not contraction. But the falsifiers are precise: jobless claims through 300k and high-yield spreads above 450bp would flip the board fast — those are the two numbers to watch.

questions.question: Can the US ever pay off its debt?
questions.answer: Not in the literal sense — and the experts (conviction 25/100 on sustainability) argue it won't try. The historical playbook is financial repression: hold rates below inflation and shrink the debt in real terms. That regime favors real assets over paper claims.

questions.question: Should I hedge against dollar devaluation?
questions.answer: The experts disagree on the dollar itself (54/100) but overlap on the hedge: scarce assets. That's why hard assets score 90/100 while the dollar call sits at a split — you don't need to win the FX debate to own the debasement hedge.

questions.question: How do I invest in longevity?
questions.answer: The most honest answer on this page: the science is rated 87/100 by our panel (568 claims — the deepest health cluster we track), but there is no clean public-market vehicle yet. It leads our 'frontier with no vehicle' list. Adjacent exposure today: GLP-1 franchises (Gap +21), biotech platforms, and diagnostics — when a pure vehicle appears, the Gap will be born wide.

questions.question: How do I get AI exposure without buying chip stocks?
questions.answer: The expert answer: own the constraint, not the crowd. Power and datacenter infrastructure (82/100, Gap +48) and the application/agent layer (Gap +76) express the same buildout at a fraction of the chip trade's 98th-percentile extension. Energy & commodities (69/100) is the second-derivative play.

questions.question: How are the experts positioned for 2026?
questions.answer: The board in one line: long scarcity (hard assets 90), long the AI buildout's unpriced layers (infrastructure Gap +48), skeptical of duration (bonds 28) — with equities constructive but selective (66/100). Our model portfolios translate exactly this into holdings, fully public.

questions.question: Is AI a bubble?
questions.answer: Not uniformly — and the nuance is measurable. Our science panel rates AI feasibility 84/100, yet the power/datacenter trade sits at only the 35th percentile of its own price history (Frontier Gap +48). The crowding is concentrated in chips: the semiconductor basket trades at its 98th percentile (Gap -13). Translation: the buildout is real; the bubble risk lives in its most crowded expression.

questions.question: Is AI capex sustainable?
questions.answer: The bull case (82/100): compute demand still overwhelms supply — 'no idle GPUs in the world.' The strongest caution in our KB: the ~$4T AI-IPO wave may mark a near-term capex-trade peak needing months of digestion. The falsifier is precise: two-plus hyperscalers cutting capex guidance in consecutive quarters.

questions.question: Is Bitcoin a good investment in 2026?
questions.answer: Expert conviction on Bitcoin's store-of-value thesis runs 89/100 among the voices we track, tied tightly to the liquidity call (82/100 that a new injection is coming) — BTC is the purest expression of that thesis. The honest caveat: our science-panel sample here is still small, so we publish direction, not a Gap.

questions.question: Is the crypto bull market over?
questions.answer: The board's honest answer: it hinges on liquidity, not vibes. The bull case (82/100): a policy-forced injection recycles into speculative assets. The strongest bear voice in our KB sees no near-term impulse — 'a stagflationary setup where crypto bleeds first.' L1 conviction is 89/100 with the trade washed out at its 19th percentile.

questions.question: Is the Fed going to print money again?
questions.answer: Expert conviction that a new liquidity injection is coming: 82/100. The mechanism isn't ideology — it's plumbing: the RRP buffer is drained, reserves are tightening, and debt service forces the balance sheet back into play within 6–18 months on their timeline.

questions.question: Is the GLP-1 hype justified by the science?
questions.answer: Per our frontier-science panel: largely yes — feasibility 76/100 across 443 claims, with expanding evidence beyond weight loss (cardiovascular, kidney, addiction pathways under study). The open questions the panel flags: long-term adherence, muscle-loss management, and payer economics.

questions.question: Is gold still a good investment in 2026?
questions.answer: By expert conviction, yes — 90/100 with only 10% of weighted voices on the bear side. The regime driver (fiscal dominance) hasn't resolved; the falsifier to watch is a credible move toward deficits below ~3.5% of GDP.

questions.question: Is inflation coming back in 2026?
questions.answer: Genuinely split — 51/100, but the trend is rising: the six-month drift favors the sticky camp, which sees a 3–4% structural floor under fiscal dominance rather than a clean return to 2%.

questions.question: Is labor scarcity permanent?
questions.answer: Leaning yes but contested (57/100): fertility collapse and boomer retirement are already locked into the worker pipeline. The live counterargument — AI obsoleting white-collar work fast enough to flip scarcity into surplus — is the main reason this isn't 80+.

questions.question: Is neurotech investable?
questions.answer: The science panel rates neurotech/BCI feasibility 96/100 — among the highest on our board — but there is no clean public vehicle: it sits on our 'frontier with no vehicle' list. That combination (science nearly certain, market access nonexistent) is precisely what becomes a wide Gap the day a vehicle lists.

questions.question: Is nuclear power making a comeback?
questions.answer: Per the distilled expert view: yes, dragged back by arithmetic — AI datacenters need firm, dense, carbon-free power, and nuclear is the only technology that is all three. It rides the energy call (69/100) and the AI-power bottleneck thesis (82/100); SMR timelines remain the honest uncertainty.

questions.question: Is Nvidia still a buy?
questions.answer: Field-level honesty first: expert conviction on AI compute is 86/100 — the science is real — but the chip trade sits at its 98th percentile of 10-year price history (Frontier Gap -13): the most crowded expression of a true story. Our full NVDA deep dive (fair-value range, scores, verdict) is linked below.

questions.question: Is passive investing distorting the market?
questions.answer: A genuine cluster in our KB (1,306 claims on market structure): the flows-driven view holds that passive bid-and-forget flows amplify concentration and momentum — one reason mega-cap dominance persists beyond fundamentals. Conviction on the structural-distortion thesis runs 66/100; the practical takeaway the experts draw is that flows, not fundamentals, set short-run prices.

questions.question: Is quantum computing investable yet?
questions.answer: Here's our honesty policy in action: expert conviction runs 64/100, but our science panel has only a handful of graded quantum claims — below our 15-claim minimum, so we publish no feasibility number and no Gap. What the thin sample suggests: real physics, unclear timelines, extremely volatile pure-plays. It's on the watch list, not the board.

questions.question: Is the 60/40 portfolio dead?
questions.answer: The experts we track have effectively rebuilt it: the '40' is the problem (long-bond conviction: 28/100 — bonds no longer hedge inflationary shocks, they ARE the taxed asset). The distilled replacement: real assets and bills take much of the bond sleeve's job (hard assets 90/100). Same diversification goal, different tools.

questions.question: Is the AI trade over?
questions.answer: The distilled expert view: the first chapter (chips) is fully priced — that basket is at its 98th percentile. The next chapters (power, applications) are not: infrastructure carries a Frontier Gap of +48. Trades rotate; the buildout continues.

questions.question: Is the dollar going to collapse?
questions.answer: No consensus — and that's the honest answer: 54/100 with 46% disagreement, the closest thing to a coin-flip on our board. Debasement (Gromen) vs the dollar-milkshake squeeze (Johnson) — position sizing, not direction, is the actionable output.

questions.question: Is TLT a good investment in 2026?
questions.answer: Our tracked experts are skeptical: 28/100 conviction on long bonds. TLT is the deflation-tail hedge — it pays off in a bust, and costs you in the sticky-inflation base case most of them expect.

questions.question: Is the US debt a real problem?
questions.answer: The tracked experts' conviction that the fiscal path is sustainable: 25/100 — a strong no. With interest consuming roughly a third of receipts, their base case isn't default but debasement: the debt gets inflated away, which is a tax on cash and bonds.

questions.question: Should I buy altcoins now?
questions.answer: The experts we track distinguish sharply: majors with real usage (L1s at 89/100, washed out at the 19th percentile) versus the long tail, where most tokens never recover their prior cycle highs. The distilled rule: liquidity rallies lift quality first — breadth comes late or never.

questions.question: Should I buy long-term bonds now?
questions.answer: The experts we track say no — conviction on owning duration is just 28/100. Sticky inflation plus exploding Treasury supply is the case against; the one scenario that flips it is a genuine recession (watch claims above 300k and HY spreads above 450bp).

questions.question: Are Ethereum and Solana good investments now?
questions.answer: Tracked-expert conviction on the smart-contract layer: 89/100, cooling — and the trade is washed out, sitting near the 19th percentile of its price history (Frontier Gap +64: high feasibility, low pricing).

questions.question: Should I buy gold right now?
questions.answer: The tracked experts' conviction on hard assets sits at 90/100 — the strongest call on our board. Their case: persistent deficits force debasement, and scarce assets are the release valve. The honest caveat: after a huge run, entry timing is yours — conviction measures the thesis, not this week's price.

questions.question: Should I invest in AI infrastructure stocks?
questions.answer: The expert case is strong — Synthos Score 82/100, rising — because power, not chips, is the binding constraint on AI. And unlike semis (at their 98th pctile), the infrastructure basket sits at just its 35th percentile — real science, un-run trade (Frontier Gap +48).

questions.question: Should I invest in space stocks?
questions.answer: The science panel rates the space economy 84/100 (launch costs collapsing, real satellite economics) with the trade at its 68th percentile — a Frontier Gap of +16. Expert conviction: 87/100, rising. Thin public universe (launch + defense-adjacent names) means concentration risk is the price of entry.

questions.question: Should investors worry about US–China decoupling?
questions.answer: It's the single most-debated topic in our knowledge base (4,539 expert claims). Conviction that fragmentation is structural: 61/100. The investable translation: an inflation floor, a reshoring capex supercycle, and supply-chain risk premia — a regime to position for, not a headline to trade.

questions.question: What are the biggest risks to markets right now?
questions.answer: We publish our falsifiers, so this one's precise: (1) two-plus hyperscalers cutting AI capex in consecutive quarters — breaks the buildout calls; (2) jobless claims through 300k with HY spreads over 450bp — recession flips the board to duration; (3) a core-CPI reacceleration above 4% — forces the Fed against the liquidity call. Each is pre-committed on the relevant prediction page.

questions.question: What assets protect against inflation?
questions.answer: The expert hierarchy in our KB: scarce monetary assets first (gold/hard assets, 90/100), real-asset cash flows second (energy & commodities, 69/100), then productive equities with pricing power — while long nominal bonds (28/100) are the asset inflation taxes. Bitcoin (89/100) is the newer entrant with the shorter track record.

questions.question: What do longevity scientists actually agree on?
questions.answer: From 568 distilled claims, the consensus core is unglamorous: VO2max, muscle mass and strength, sleep, and metabolic health dominate lifespan outcomes — the panel's conviction concentrates on exercise as the single largest lever, with GLP-1s as the biggest pharmacological shift. The frontier (senolytics, reprogramming) rates promising-but-early. Not medical advice; it is, however, where the invest

questions.question: What does currency debasement mean for my savings?
questions.answer: Debasement is a slow tax on cash and fixed-income: your money keeps its number and loses its meaning. The experts we track (90/100 on hard assets, 25/100 that the fiscal path is sustainable) see it as the chosen release valve for the debt — which is why scarce assets anchor their allocations.

questions.question: What will drive the next crypto cycle?
questions.answer: Three distilled drivers, in order: global liquidity (the 82/100 injection call — the master variable), stablecoin/tokenization adoption (89/100), and the halving-supply mechanics that amplify whatever demand arrives. The experts treat the first as necessary; the other two as multipliers.

questions.question: What happens to markets when QT ends?
questions.answer: The distilled expert playbook: liquidity injections recycle into scarce and speculative assets first — historically gold, Bitcoin, and high-beta growth front-run the turn. Conviction the turn is coming: 82/100. That's the mechanism linking the liquidity call to the crypto and hard-asset calls.

questions.question: What is fiscal dominance?
questions.answer: Fiscal dominance is when government debt gets so large that monetary policy stops serving inflation control and starts serving the Treasury's funding needs. Our experts put sustainability conviction at 25/100 — meaning they believe we're already in it. It's the single assumption underneath the gold (90), bonds (28), and liquidity (82) calls.

questions.question: What's the most contrarian trade right now?
questions.answer: By our own numbers, two: long duration (bonds at 28/100 — everyone hates them; a recession makes the crowd wrong) and fading the chip trade (real science, 98th-percentile crowding, Gap -13). Contrarian isn't a strategy — but knowing where consensus is fully priced is.

questions.question: Where do the best-graded investors have conviction now?
questions.answer: Cross-referencing the honor roll (Verified Voices — forecasters whose matured calls beat their field baseline) with the live board: the buildout's real-asset layer (power 82, energy 69), scarce assets (90), and the liquidity turn (82). Every graded voice's weight already flows into these scores — that's the system.

questions.question: When will AGI arrive?
questions.answer: Our feasibility panel rates foundation-model progress 70/100 — real but decelerating debate, with 18% dissent and honest disagreement on timelines (the tracked range runs from 'scaling is hitting walls' to 'imminent'). We publish the dispersion rather than a fake point estimate; what's measurable is that capital is behaving as if it's close (see the compute and power calls).

questions.question: Where is expert conviction highest in AI?
questions.answer: Measured, not vibes: compute/chips carries the most discussion (4,956 claims, 86/100) but trades at its 98th percentile — while infrastructure & power (82/100, 35th percentile, Gap +48) and applications/agents (Gap +76) offer the same story with far less crowding. The 586 deep dives carry per-name verdicts.

questions.question: Who wins the AI race — the US or China?
questions.answer: The distilled view isn't a winner — it's a dynamic: game theory forces both powers to funnel capital into AI regardless of returns, because falling behind is the only unacceptable outcome. That's bullish compute demand (86/100) and bullish fragmentation (61/100) simultaneously — the race itself is the trade.

questions.question: Why are commodities going up?
questions.answer: Three distilled drivers: the AI power buildout (structural new demand), reshoring capex, and years of underinvested supply. Expert conviction on the complex: 69/100.

questions.question: Why do experts prefer gold over bonds right now?
questions.answer: The widest spread on our board: hard assets 90/100 vs long bonds 28/100. Same macro view, two verdicts — if deficits get inflated away, bonds are the asset being taxed and gold is the asset collecting it.

questions.question: Why is gold going up?
questions.answer: The distilled expert answer: governments must fund deficits that politics won't shrink, so policymakers choose currency debasement over bond-market failure — and gold is the hedge that has no issuer. Expert conviction: 90/100.

questions.question: Will AI cause deflation or inflation?
questions.answer: The KB's sharpest exchange: the disinflation camp says AI productivity delivers 2% faster than anyone expects; the sticky camp answers that 'AI disinflation is a fairy tale — datacenters cost more, not less,' and the buildout is itself an energy and capex shock. The inflation call sits split at 51/100, trend rising — toward the sticky camp.

questions.question: Will AI take my job?
questions.answer: The honest expert split: the feasibility panel rates transformative AI applications 91/100 real, and voices like Goertzel argue most white-collar tasks are automatable — but the demographic counterweight (worker shortage from collapsed fertility) means labor scarcity and AI displacement are colliding forces. Watch wage growth: 4+ quarters of AI-suppressed wages would settle it.

questions.question: Will AI transform drug discovery?
questions.answer: The science panel says yes — feasibility 87/100 — with the honest caveat that biology's bottleneck is trials, not ideas: AI compresses the discovery phase while the expensive human-testing years remain. Platform conviction: 83/100; the trade note is that the biotech-platform basket already sits at its 98th percentile.

questions.question: Will Bitcoin hit $200k?
questions.answer: We don't do magic numbers — price targets are astrology with extra steps. What we measure: store-of-value conviction at 89/100, and the driver that matters (82/100 conviction a liquidity injection is coming — historically what moves BTC hardest). If the driver fires, magnitude takes care of itself; if it doesn't, no target survives.

questions.question: Will bond yields come down?
questions.answer: The weighted expert view leans no — not durably, while inflation floors near 3% and coupon issuance keeps growing. Duration bulls are only 28% of conviction-weighted opinion; the rest see the long end structurally repriced.

questions.question: Will the Fed cut rates in 2026?
questions.answer: Split — 48/100. The sharper argument our KB surfaces: it matters less than you think. Treasury issuance now moves markets more than 25bp of Fed policy, and the plumbing (reserves, RRP) will force the Fed's hand either way.

questions.question: Will inflation go back to 2%?
questions.answer: The experts are split (51/100, 49% dissent), but the stronger-conviction voices argue 2% requires deficits below 4% of GDP — which politics won't deliver — making ~3% the new floor. Watch core PCE under 2.5% with growth intact as the falsifier.

questions.question: Will QE come back in 2026?
questions.answer: Most tracked experts think some form of balance-sheet support returns — conviction 82/100 — though likely rebranded (reserve management, not 'QE'). The tell: bank reserves hitting scarcity while Treasury issuance stays heavy.

questions.question: Will tariffs cause inflation?
questions.answer: The distilled view: tariffs are one layer of a broader inflation floor — fragmentation trades efficiency for security, and somebody pays the difference. The inflation call sits split at 51/100 but rising, and the geopolitics call (61/100) says the driver isn't going away.
